metadata.title: Game Design Theory: A New Philosophy (Burgun) — Book Review

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Original Tetris had a single next-piece preview, true randomness, no hold box, and time pressure on every piece. Modern Tetris added a 6-piece preview, hold box, easy spin, and a 7-Bag generator that removes true randomness. Burgun's analyses are consistent with his framework but function as design critiques, not empirical findings. None of the prescriptions have actually been tested, and the book is strongest when it admits that.

artifactLines: Original Tetris had a single next-piece preview, true randomness, no hold box, and time pressure on every piece.
Modern Tetris added a 6-piece preview, hold box, easy spin, and a 7-Bag generator that removes true randomness.
Burgun's analyses are consistent with his framework but function as design critiques, not empirical findings.
None of the prescriptions have actually been tested, and the book is strongest when it admits that.

beats.narration_text: The contrast between original and modern Tetris is a perfect lens, and I want to use it to test Burgun's framework myself. Help me (1) map each modern addition — 6-piece preview, hold box, easy spin, 7-Bag generator — to exactly what it removes from the original's decision-making, (2) turn one of Burgun's untested prescriptions into an actual playtest hypothesis I could measure, and (3) name where his design critique stops being a finding and becomes just a consistent opinion.
